content.text: Why Capacity Must Constrain the Portfolio

A prioritised backlog is not a plan. Without explicit capacity modelling, organisations routinely commit to more GenAI projects than their teams can deliver — then blame "poor execution" when timelines slip. Capacity-aware planning makes the constraint visible at the portfolio level, before commitments are made to stakeholders, so that the plan is achievable by design.
Never plan to 100 % of capacity. Scope creep, incidents, and onboarding overhead consume 20–30 % of every team's time. A plan that fills every slot is a plan that will miss deadlines.
Quarterly Planning Cycle

The planner takes three inputs — a demand list sorted by priority score, a capacity model for the quarter, and a set of hard constraints — and greedily fills the committed plan until capacity is exhausted. Projects that do not fit enter a deferred queue, pre-scored and ready for the next quarter without re-triage.
Modelling Capacity
Capacity for GenAI work is measured in
person-weeks
(or story-points calibrated to a team's historical velocity). The gross capacity of a team for a 13-week quarter is:
Estimating Project Effort

Each project in the demand list needs an effort estimate in the same units as the capacity model (person-weeks). Estimates at this planning stage are rough — the goal is order-of-magnitude accuracy (±30 %), not detailed planning. Use a three-point estimate to capture uncertainty:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ProjectEstimate:
    request_id: str
    optimistic_pw: float    # best case, person-weeks
    likely_pw: float        # most likely
    pessimistic_pw: float   # worst case

    def expected_pw(self) -> float:
        """PERT expected value: (O + 4M + P) / 6"""
        return (self.optimistic_pw + 4 * self.likely_pw + self.pessimistic_pw) / 6

    def std_dev_pw(self) -> float:
        """PERT standard deviation: (P - O) / 6"""
        return (self.pessimistic_pw - self.optimistic_pw) / 6

    def skill_breakdown(self) -> dict[str, float]:
        """Override this per project with actual skill mix."""
        # Default: 40 % ML, 30 % data, 20 % platform, 10 % QA
        e = self.expected_pw()
        return {
            "ml_engineer":       e * 0.40,
            "data_engineer":     e * 0.30,
            "platform_engineer": e * 0.20,
            "qa_engineer":       e * 0.10,
        }
The Portfolio Filling Algorithm

With demand and capacity both expressed in person-weeks, filling the portfolio is a constrained selection problem. The greedy algorithm — take the highest-scoring project that fits within remaining capacity — is sufficient for most organisations. A full integer linear program is only warranted when there are strict skill-mix constraints across dozens of simultaneous projects.
Skill-Constraint Checking

The simple capacity fill above treats all person-weeks as interchangeable. In practice, you may have plenty of ML engineer hours but no data engineer hours — committing a data-heavy project will stall regardless of total person-week headroom. Add a skill-level check after the initial fill:
Budget Constraint Integration
Capacity in person-weeks is the primary constraint for internally-staffed work. Infrastructure and API costs add a
financial capacity
constraint for GenAI projects specifically. Add a budget check in parallel with the person-week check:

@dataclass
class BudgetEstimate:
    request_id: str
    build_cost_usd: float       # one-time: engineering time + tooling setup
    quarterly_opex_usd: float   # recurring: API calls, compute, licenses

def add_budget_constraint(
    plan: PortfolioPlan,
    estimates: dict[str, BudgetEstimate],
    quarterly_budget_usd: float,
) -> list[str]:
    """
    Return list of request_ids that breach the budget ceiling.
    Caller should move these from committed to deferred.
    """
    cumulative = 0.0
    over_budget: list[str] = []
    for slot in plan.committed:
        est = estimates.get(slot.request_id)
        if est is None:
            continue
        cumulative += est.build_cost_usd + est.quarterly_opex_usd
        if cumulative > quarterly_budget_usd:
            over_budget.append(slot.request_id)
    return over_budget
Planning Cadence and Mid-Quarter Adjustments
Quarterly planning is the primary cadence, but the plan must be revisitable when reality changes:
Trigger
Action
Process
New Tier 1 request arrives mid-quarter
Insert into plan if capacity allows; else defer to Q+1
Emergency triage; COE decision within 2 days
Project scope expands > 30 %
Re-estimate and re-check capacity; may need to defer another project
Re-estimate with PM; COE approval
Team member leaves or goes on extended leave
Recalculate net capacity; may need to slide commitments
Capacity recalc in planning tool
Mandatory regulatory item added
Insert regardless of capacity; identify which discretionary project to defer
Immediate COE notification
Project delivered early
Pull in next deferred project if > 3 weeks remain in quarter
PM confirms readiness; COE approves
Portfolio Health Metrics
Track these at the end of each quarter to improve future planning accuracy:
Common Failure Modes
Planning to 100 % utilisation
: leaves no room for scope creep, incidents, or onboarding. Target 70–80 % of net capacity.
Ignoring skill mix
: total person-weeks look fine but all projects are ML-heavy and there is only one ML engineer. The bottleneck collapses the plan.
Forgetting carry-forward work
: projects started in a previous quarter that are still in progress consume current-quarter capacity. Always deduct in-flight work before filling new slots.
No mid-quarter re-plan trigger
: waiting until next quarter to acknowledge a departed team member or blown scope is too late. Define explicit triggers for unscheduled re-planning.
Treating estimates as commitments
: rough estimates at planning time have ±30 % uncertainty. The plan must absorb variance; teams should not be held to three-point estimates made 13 weeks earlier without re-baselining.
Communicating the Plan to Stakeholders
The output of capacity-aware planning is a commitment — and commitments need to be communicated clearly. Two audiences need different views:
Executive View

Senior leaders need a one-page portfolio summary showing: how many projects are committed vs deferred, total investment committed for the quarter, expected value delivery, and the top-three risks to the plan. Do not show person-weeks or skill mix at this level — convert to outcomes.
Team Lead View

Engineering and delivery leads need the committed project list sorted by start order, with skill requirements and expected person-week draw per sprint. This view drives sprint planning in the first week of the quarter.

def build_team_delivery_view(
    plan: PortfolioPlan,
    capacity: QuarterCapacity,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Return one dict per committed project in recommended start order."""
    skill_remaining = dict(capacity.net_by_skill())
    output = []
    for i, slot in enumerate(plan.committed):
        skills = slot.estimate.skill_breakdown()
        # Deduct from remaining to surface sequential bottlenecks
        for sk, pw in skills.items():
            skill_remaining[sk] = skill_remaining.get(sk, 0) - pw
        output.append({
            "rank": i + 1,
            "request_id": slot.request_id,
            "expected_pw": round(slot.estimate.expected_pw(), 1),
            "skill_mix_pw": {k: round(v, 1) for k, v in skills.items()},
            "skill_remaining_after_pw": {k: round(v, 1) for k, v in skill_remaining.items()},
            "mandatory": slot.is_mandatory,
        })
    return output
Rolling Capacity Model — Keeping It Current
A quarterly capacity model built once at the start of the quarter decays as reality diverges. Maintain a rolling model that refreshes on four triggers:
Headcount change
: new hire, departure, or secondment. Update the
team_members
list within one business day.
Availability change
: a team member's allocation to the GenAI programme changes (another project takes priority). Re-run the capacity and skill-check immediately.
Project completion
: a committed project finishes early. Freed person-weeks may allow pulling in the top deferred project if ≥ 3 weeks remain in the quarter.
Scope expansion
: a committed project re-estimates upward by > 30 %. Re-run the portfolio fill to check if another project must be deferred to absorb the overrun.

from datetime import date

@dataclass
class CapacitySnapshot:
    """Immutable snapshot of capacity at a point in time."""
    snapshot_date: date
    quarter: str
    net_person_weeks: float
    net_by_skill: dict[str, float]
    change_reason: str   # why this snapshot was taken

def record_capacity_snapshot(
    capacity: QuarterCapacity,
    reason: str,
    as_of: date | None = None,
) -> CapacitySnapshot:
    if as_of is None:
        as_of = date.today()
    return CapacitySnapshot(
        snapshot_date    = as_of,
        quarter          = capacity.quarter,
        net_person_weeks = round(capacity.net_person_weeks(), 1),
        net_by_skill     = {k: round(v, 1) for k, v in capacity.net_by_skill().items()},
        change_reason    = reason,
    )
# Keep a list of snapshots per quarter to audit how capacity changed
# and correlate with delivery rate deviations at retrospective.
Summary

Capacity-aware portfolio planning prevents over-commitment by translating team headcount into net deliverable person-weeks (gross × availability × (1 − leave − support − innovation buffers)) and greedily filling the committed plan up to 70–80 % of that net capacity, sorted by priority score. Mandatory items (regulatory mandates) bypass the capacity check. Skill-level feasibility checks catch bottlenecks that total person-week headroom misses. Budget constraints add a second axis for GenAI-specific API and compute costs. Planning health is measured post-quarter by delivery rate, capacity accuracy, and budget accuracy — and fed back to improve next-quarter estimates.
